data.title: Tough penalties planned to stop unapproved fire system shutdowns in Hong Kong
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have proposed new offences with tougher punishments for fire installation contractors who fail to obtain prior approval before deactivating firefighting systems or do not report malfunctions within 24 hours, following the city’s deadliest blaze in decades, the South China Morning Post has learned.
Other proposed changes included drastically increasing the maximum penalty for existing offences related to the unauthorised handling of fire equipment and the issuance of false or misleading certificates, from the original HK$50,000 (US$6,380) fine to a maximum fine of HK$3 million and three years’ imprisonment.
Hearings by a judge-led independent inquiry into last November’s deadly blaze at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court exposed a series of failures and alleged malpractice by various parties, which led to the deactivation of fire alarms during renovations.
The SCMP learned on Wednesday that the Security Bureau proposed a set of legal amendments to improve oversight of fire installation contractors, including requiring prior approval before deactivating firefighting systems, such as hose reels and fire alarms.
Under existing regulations, contractors are only required to submit a shutdown notice to authorities after deactivating such systems.
Contractors would also be in breach of regulations if they failed to notify the Fire Services Department of any malfunctioning firefighting systems within 24 hours, display annual fire inspection certificates or implement temporary fire safety measures.
Those found to have breached the new laws for the first time could be fined up to HK$200,000, with an additional HK$20,000 for every further day of non-compliance.
For subsequent convictions, they could face an escalated fine of HK$400,000, with an additional daily penalty of up to HK$40,000, and a year in prison.
Another new offence will target any personnel who deliberately or recklessly misuse or interfere with fire equipment, with offenders facing a fine of HK$1 million and a one-year jail term.
But for serious cases resulting in severe casualties and property losses, the offence will carry a maximum penalty of three years’ imprisonment and a fine of HK$3 million.




The government also suggested more stringent punishments for existing offences, including unauthorised installation, inspection and repair of fire equipment by non-licensed personnel, and the signing and issuance of false or misleading certificates.
The amendment proposed a maximum penalty of a HK$3 million fine and three years’ imprisonment, up from the current HK$50,000 fine.
A source said the proposed legislation would hold property management companies legally liable for ensuring fire safety, in addition to owners’ corporations.
Liable parties failing to ensure the operability of firefighting systems or not conducting periodic inspections could face a maximum fine of HK$500,000, a tenfold increase from the current HK$50,000.
The source said the government would also introduce fixed penalties ranging from HK$1,500 to HK$6,000 for certain violations, including failure to display annual fire inspection certificates and implement the required temporary fire safety measures.
Other offences punishable by fixed penalties would include blocking or locking means of escape or failing to comply with fire hazard abatement notices.
Victor Dawes, lead counsel for the independent committee, highlighted that the failure of fire alarms across the eight blocks of Wang Fuk Court was one of the six “human factors” that contributed to the high number of deaths in the fire.
The inquiry heard in recent weeks that employees of ISS EastPoint, the estate’s property management company, who were not licensed fire services personnel, switched off the main power supply controlling the hose reel system to allow maintenance of the fire water tanks, and were unaware that this move had also deactivated the fire alarms.

China Status Development and Engineering, hired by the estate’s renovation contractor, Prestige Construction and Engineering, was found to have filed 85 shutdown notices to deactivate the fire hydrant and hose reel system across all eight blocks from as early as April 7 last year, despite failing to visit the site to assess the necessity of such a move.
Victory Fire Engineering, the contractor responsible for maintaining the estate’s fire services equipment, also failed to notify authorities when they discovered that the hose reel system and fire alarms had been turned off a week before the blaze.
Lawmaker Mark Chong Ho-fung said he expected the legal changes to better deter malpractice. He emphasised that property management companies should step up frontline inspections to ensure that firefighting systems were in place.
“For example, regarding the blockage of fire escapes, by the time firefighters discover it during inspection, the situation is already critical and has likely persisted for a long time,” he said. “But the building attendant patrols every day, and they should have spotted it.”
Chong also called on authorities to take swift and decisive enforcement action upon receiving reports of breaches.
“If you pass a law but remain loose in its enforcement, the legislation will be useless,” the lawmaker said.
The proposed amendments are set to be discussed at a Legislative Council security panel meeting on May 5.
The bureau has also proposed a series of amendments to curb illegal refuelling amid surging global oil prices by targeting buyers of illicit fuels in the city.
Under the planned legislation, offenders will face a maximum penalty of HK$1 million and one year in prison.

data.title: Hong Kong-Shenzhen tech cooperation gets a boost with Lenovo AI lab in Hetao hub
data.description:
Hong Kong’s technology cooperation with Shenzhen received a boost on Wednesday as Lenovo Group, the world’s biggest personal computer maker, set up an innovation lab at the city’s new Hetao tech hub and pledged to help firms adopt artificial intelligence and expand globally.
Lenovo is the first multinational tech firm to publicly announce its presence on the Hong Kong side of the Hetao zone – the Hong Kong-Shenzhen Innovation and Technology Park (HSITP) – which covers 87 hectares in the city’s northern Lok Ma Chau area.
Ken Wong, president of Lenovo’s Solutions & Services Group, said the new Lenovo AI Innovation Lab at HSITP aimed to be an “open platform” to help partners access the tech giant’s AI solutions and scale their businesses internationally.
The cross-border Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone was launched in 2017, following Beijing’s call for closer integration between the two cities.

The Beijing and Morrisville, US, dual-headquartered PC maker chose Hetao for its new AI centre as it combined Hong Kong’s advantages in research and access to the global market with the manufacturing strengths of Shenzhen and the Greater Bay Area, according to Wong.
The park was “making it easier for talent, goods, capital, and data to move across the border”, Wong said.
Lenovo said that its new AI lab had attracted more than 30 partners, including Chinese cybersecurity firms 360 Security Technology and Qi An Xin, server maker H3C and enterprise management systems firm Yonyou.
Lenovo’s addition to HSITP would help attract more companies to set up in Hong Kong to serve global markets, said Vincent Ma, CEO of HSITP.
The Hong Kong side of the Hetao tech hub officially commenced operations in December. Some 60 enterprises had already moved into its two first-phase buildings, representing an 80 per cent occupancy rate. It complements a 302-hectare site on the Shenzhen side.
The city has high hopes for HSITP, which forms part of the Northern Metropolis, a flagship initiative aimed at strengthening Hong Kong’s economic base and deepening integration with the Greater Bay Area. Beijing’s newly unveiled 15th five-year plan urged development of the project.
The Hetao park in Hong Kong aimed to become a “world-class innovation powerhouse”, said Secretary for Innovation, Technology and Industry Sun Dong.
The Asian financial hub has in recent years set about revamping itself as an innovation and technology centre.
The city has started construction on a major supercomputing data centre, which Sun unveiled last June. It is set to provide 180,000 petaflops of computing power, a 36-fold increase from the current level.

data.title: China’s low-cost EVs to be fitted with lidar systems, usually saved for lux cars
data.description:
China’s electric vehicle (EV) makers could introduce a lidar sensor-based intelligent driving system in models priced as low as 60,000 yuan (US$8,795) this year, reflecting intensifying competition and falling costs, according to AI-driven tech firm Robosense.
This price point is the lowest for models on sale in China’s market, excluding mini vehicles sold for less than 50,000 yuan, indicating fierce competition among Chinese carmakers amid the phasing out of policy incentives.
Notably, light detection and ranging (lidar) systems were historically reserved for high-end models or premium trims, as they are a cornerstone of advanced automotive safety due to their ability to provide precise road data during extreme conditions, such as heavy fog. This exclusivity was largely driven by the high manufacturing costs of components.
However, the landscape has shifted dramatically due to rapid technological advancements and the efficiencies of large-scale production.
The average selling price (ASP) of automotive lidar sensors – which sat at around US$80,000 in 2016 – plummeted to US$200 by 2025. This transition has also seen a shift in global leadership, with Chinese manufacturers overtaking US firms as the dominant market players.
By comparison, the lidar units used by companies in the United States range from US$1,000 to US$75,000.
Manufacturers are increasingly integrating lidar into budget-friendly models amid an aggressive price war in the Chinese EV sector. While these systems were seen in vehicles priced between 100,000 and 200,000 yuan in 2025, the floor has continued to drop.
This was evidenced in March 2026, when Leapmotor launched a new model equipped with the technology priced at just 86,800 yuan.


Current trends suggest the threshold is set to drop further to the 60,000 yuan mark, as a wave of Chinese EV makers are expected to soon announce the adoption of a lidar sensor solution as a standard feature for all models of some series, said Mark Qiu Chunchao, CEO of RoboSense.
Qiu predicted that RoboSense’s latest self-developed Phoenix chipset – which allows the lidar sensors to detect items as far as 600 meters (1,970 feet) away – could be used in 60,000-yuan EVs within two years. He did not reveal the product’s selling price.
“To be honest, I don’t really care whether ASP is going to plummet; I just care about how big this pie can get,” Qiu told the South China Morning Post.
He believed that scaling up sales could offset the decline in gross profit margins and that technological upgrades would result in lower costs.
Inexpensive EVs are growing more popular in mainland China. Last year, models priced below 80,000 yuan recorded a sales volume of 1.53 million units and those priced between 80,000 yuan and 100,000 yuan logged 1.49 million units, up by 51.8 per cent and 78.4 per cent year-on-year, respectively.
However, growth across all price bands also rose by 28.2 per cent, data from the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers showed.
Competition among Chinese lidar makers is intensifying, with all three industry leaders – Huawei Technologies, Hesai Group and RoboSense – unveiling breakthrough hardware within a seven-week window.
Over a span of four days, both Hesai and RoboSense announced they would launch their colour-capable lidar sensor, which makes the component work more like a camera while carrying more data.

data.title: Hong Kong, Malaysia exchanges to team up on ETFs, listings, Islamic finance
data.description:
The bourse operators of Hong Kong and Malaysia are exploring a range of collaborations, from dual listings and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) to Islamic finance, to forge closer ties between the city and the Southeast Asian country.
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) CEO Bonnie Chan Yiting and Boursa Malaysia CEO Dato Fad’l Mohamed unveiled their joint development plans in a media briefing in Hong Kong on Wednesday as HKEX hosted the 40th Asian and Oceanian Stock Exchanges Federation (AOSEF) General Assembly at HKEX Connect Hall in Exchange Square in Central.
In the first major collaboration with Boursa Malaysia, HKEX has granted Da Cheng International Asset Management a licence to issue an ETF based on the HKEX Bursa Malaysia Large Cap Index, which tracks scores of the largest companies listed in Hong Kong and Malaysia, with 62 per cent Hong Kong stocks and 38 per cent Malaysian shares.
“This development underscores the growing demand from investors for cross-border products and diversified regional exposure and highlights how collaboration between exchanges can directly support market innovation,” Chan said.
The ETF will be launched and listed in Hong Kong after regulatory approval, and mainland investors will be able to invest in the fund via the ETF Connect scheme.
Mohamed said the two exchanges could also collaborate on dual listings, as Malaysia would welcome listings by Hong Kong and mainland firms. About 30 Malaysian companies were currently listed in Hong Kong, accounting for almost a third of 103 Southeast Asian issuers, HKEX data showed. The biggest player is Guoco Group, which is part of the Hong Leong Group, a Malaysian conglomerate.
“It will be a win-win for both sides, because it helps companies that are already present in markets such as Hong Kong and mainland China to be able to tap into liquidity pools in Malaysia,” he said. “It also gives an opportunity for Malaysian companies that are already listed on Bursa Malaysia to be able to have a secondary listing in Hong Kong.”
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po said HKEX had agreements with 20 exchanges worldwide to facilitate dual listings of equities and bonds.

“Global institutional investors are actively seeking diversification and long-term growth,” he said at the event. “They are looking for Asia’s next generation of enterprises”, including green-energy innovators, digital banks and advanced manufacturers.
“Hong Kong’s role is to ensure that when that capital arrives, it flows efficiently to where Asia’s best opportunities are, wherever they may be listed.”
Mohamed said that collaboration between Malaysia and Hong Kong provided more choice for investors seeking to diversify their investments amid geopolitical uncertainties.
“What we are doing today brings together two complementary markets and marks the start of the journey where investors can view Malaysia and Hong Kong not as stand-alone markets, but as part of a connected Asian market ecosystem,” he said.
HKEX’s Chan said the collaboration would also help Hong Kong develop Islamic finance with support from experts at Bursa Malaysia.
As of January, Malaysia’s Islamic capital market accounted for 2.79 trillion ringgit (US$706 billion), representing 63 per cent of the total Malaysian capital market.
“Bursa Malaysia as a platform that connects companies and sharia-compliant investments with international investors, while serving as a gateway for companies seeking access to [Southeast Asia’s] growth markets,” Mohamed said.
The three-day AOSEF event brings together representatives from 18 exchanges across the region, including Shanghai, Shenzhen, Japan, South Korea, Indonesia and Malaysia.
HKEX chairman Carlson Tong Ka-shing said much had changed since Hong Kong last hosted the meeting 17 years ago.
“The current macro environment is as complex and challenging now as it has ever been in the last couple of decades,” Tong said. “But what stands out is that Asia’s economic presence on the global stage has grown significantly since then.”
Intraregional investment in Asia tripled over the past decade to around US$1.7 trillion in 2024, according to HKEX data.
“With global capital pivoting towards Asia, exchanges across our region have a unique opportunity to strengthen cross-market connectivity and shape the next chapter of robust and sustainable growth,” Tong said.

data.title: Hong Kong-Shenzhen tech cooperation gets a boost with Lenovo AI lab in Hetao hub
data.description:

data.title: DeepSeek isn’t short of cash: so why has it decided to raise funds anyway?
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence start-up DeepSeek has begun raising external capital for the first time, but is keeping its initial fundraising round deliberately small to limit equity dilution while retaining key talent, according to people familiar with the matter.
The Hangzhou-based company, a spin-off from hedge fund High-Flyer, is seeking to sell no more than 3 per cent of its equity, said three investors with direct knowledge of the plans, who requested anonymity as the information is private.
DeepSeek is not in urgent need of outside capital. Instead, the round was intended to establish a market benchmark for its valuation, giving employees greater clarity on the worth of their stock options – a move aimed at stemming departures amid aggressive poaching by well-funded rivals, the Hangzhou and Beijing-based investors said.
DeepSeek did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Wednesday.
Large state-backed funds were expected to participate, for example, the AI-focused affiliates of China’s so-called “Big Fund III”, one of the investors said, adding that DeepSeek’s estimated valuation could, in fact, exceed US$100 billion.
DeepSeek’s fundraising was first reported by The Information, which said the company was seeking to raise at least US$300 million at a valuation of at least US$10 billion.
The company is being highly selective in the round, according to another investor based in Hangzhou, adding that DeepSeek is prioritising state-backed and industrial investors – including local government guidance funds and affiliated platforms – favouring those that can provide strategic resources such as AI infrastructure over purely financial capital, the investor said.
Private, market-oriented funds were more likely to be included in subsequent rounds, he added, as the capital-intensive nature of AI development would require further fundraising. By then, valuations are expected to be significantly higher.
The modest size of the current fundraising reflects the strong financial position of its parent, High-Flyer, one of China’s most profitable hedge funds last year.
DeepSeek was spun off in 2023 as founder Liang Wenfeng pursued ambitions in artificial general intelligence – AI capable of matching human performance across a wide range of tasks.
In a rare interview, Liang said the company’s main constraint was not funding, but access to advanced chips amid US export controls.

High-Flyer, which manages more than 70 billion yuan (US$10.2 billion) in assets, ranked among China’s most profitable hedge funds last year, according to consultancy Simuwang.com. The firm posted an average return of 56.55 per cent in 2025, second only to Lingjun Investment.
Despite that profitability, the absence of external funding has made it difficult to gauge DeepSeek’s market valuation. The company previously rattled global markets, at one point wiping trillions of dollars from US equities.
Rivals including Beijing-based Zhipu AI and Shanghai-based MiniMax have moved ahead with public listings in Hong Kong this year, reaching a market value of HK$286 billion (US$36.5 billion) and HK$466 billion, respectively, as of Wednesday.
As competitors offer lucrative compensation packages, including stock options, DeepSeek has seen a wave of high-profile departures. Guo Daya, a lead researcher on its R1 model, was reportedly recruited by ByteDance with a package potentially worth up to 100 million yuan, including cash and equity incentives.
DeepSeek’s first external funding also comes at a time when the company appears to be lagging in the country’s heated competition for the launch of state-of-the-art AI models.
While anticipation builds around its next-generation V4 model, rivals including Moonshot AI, Zhipu AI and MiniMax have surged ahead with new releases that have drawn global attention and adoption.

data.title: Biotech-led boom as 8 China firms flock to Hong Kong’s thriving stock market
data.description:
Eight mainland China-based companies – five of them biotech firms – passed listing hearings on the Hong Kong stock market in April, clearing the way for them to tap the city’s deep liquidity.
The influx, including an AI-driven drug discovery firm, a driverless technology developer and a maker of industrial robots, comes as the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) has been pushing private firms to list in Hong Kong. The market regulator has required companies that established so-called “red-chip structures” – offshore holding vehicles typically set up in places like the Cayman Islands to control China-based businesses – before 2023 to reincorporate on the mainland and pursue Hong Kong share offerings.
Among the notable applicants is Metis TechBio, which applied under Chapter 18C, a listing regime for pre-profit specialist technology firms, according to its latest filing with the Hong Kong exchange on Sunday. Jefferies, Deutsche Bank and Citic Securities are joint sponsors of the deal.
Founded in 2020 in Hangzhou, in eastern China’s Zhejiang province, Metis operates in China and the United States. It uses three proprietary AI platforms to design and optimise drug delivery systems, a term that refers to how medicines are packaged and processed by the body.
Its revenue surged to 105 million yuan (US$15.4 million) last year from 1.48 million yuan a year earlier, while its net loss narrowed to 391.8 million yuan from 499.2 million yuan.
Its leading drug candidate, MTS-004, an orally dissolving tablet for pseudobulbar affect, has completed phase three clinical trials. The company has three other drug candidates in early-stage trials.
Average daily turnover on the Hong Kong stock exchange for the first three months of 2026 was HK$276.7 billion (US$35.3 billion), up 14 per cent from the same period last year, according to data from bourse operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX).
Mabwell Shanghai Bioscience, which has traded on the Shanghai exchange’s Star Market since January 2022, is seeking a dual listing in Hong Kong to raise up to HK$1.45 billion to fund the clinical advancement of its lead antibody-drug conjugate, according to its prospectus. It will debut on April 28.
Cofoe Medical Technology, already listed on the Shenzhen exchange, manufactures home care medical devices, while Impact Therapeutics, a commercial-stage oncology biotech firm, focuses on precision cancer therapies.
UISEE Technologies (Beijing) specialises in autonomous-driving technology for closed commercial scenarios such as airports and factories. Founded in 2016, the company has deployed its driverless vehicles with 249 customers across six countries and regions, including at the Hong Kong International Airport and Singapore Changi Airport, according to its filing on Sunday.

Also applying under Chapter 18C is Shanghai Xizhi Technology. Founded in 2017 in the United States, it develops optical interconnect and optical computing products designed to accelerate artificial intelligence infrastructure. It plans to raise HK$2.53 billion and will start trading on April 28, according to its prospectus.
RobotPhoenix Intelligent Technology, established in 2012, makes industrial robots and automated solutions for light manufacturing sectors such as automotive, electronics and semiconductors.
Healthcare has been a key driver of liquidity growth on the Hong Kong stock market over the past decade, with trading turnover for the sector surging more than 11-fold between 2015 and 2025, outpacing the technology, media and telecommunications sector, which saw a 10-fold increase over the same period, according to HKEX data.

data.title: 611 elderly Hongkongers default on student loans, owe HK$17,400 each on average
data.description:
More than 600 defaulters aged 60 and above have failed to repay their student loans under five government financial assistance schemes designed to fund their tertiary and post-secondary studies, with the average amount owed standing at HK$17,400 (US$2,220).
In a reply to questions from lawmakers on Wednesday, the Education Bureau said it had recorded 9,838 default cases, involving 8,494 people, under the five schemes as of January 31 this year.
The schemes provide financial assistance to those studying at public universities, in self-financing post-secondary programmes, pursuing professional certifications or taking part in specific continuing education courses. Some of the policies are non-means-tested.
As part of the budget for the current financial year, the Working Family and Student Financial Assistance Agency vowed to continue concerted efforts to recover student loans in arrears.
A payee is considered to have defaulted if two or more consecutive quarterly instalments are overdue. The definition also applies if six or more consecutive monthly instalments are overdue.
The latest cases recorded by the bureau involved nearly HK$126 million in default.
In comparison, it recorded 5,134 default cases involving HK$96 million as of January 31, 2025.
The surge in cases follows the resumption of student loan repayments in April last year. The government previously provided deferral arrangements between April 1, 2020, and March 31, 2025, to ease the financial burden of former students during the Covid-19 pandemic.
The bureau also provided a breakdown of defaulters for the latest figure based on their age and the average amounts owed.
Among those who defaulted, 611 are aged 60 or above – accounting for 7 per cent of the total. Those in the age group owed HK$17,300 each on average.
The number of defaulters in that demographic also rose from 553 last year, marking a 10 per cent increase.
Those aged 30 to 39 made up the largest proportion of recent defaulters at 42 per cent, or 3,561. They owed HK$11,800 on average.
But defaulters aged 40 to 49, of which there were 1,820, accounted for the highest amount owed on average, at HK$26,800.
“For student loan borrowers who apply for deferment of loan repayment, the main reasons include financial hardship, serious illness and further full-time study,” the bureau said.
It added that authorities would offer assistance, including negotiations on deferment or loan restructuring, for those who were genuinely struggling to pay back the funds.
The South China Morning Post has approached the bureau for more information on why so many elderly people were unable to repay their loans.

data.title: Confusion over government agencies’ roles in fire oversight, probe reveals
data.description:
A public inquiry into Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades has shed light on government departments’ confusion over their roles in supervising large-scale renovation projects, with a fire service official insisting his department was not responsible for handling complaints about flammable building materials because it lacked expertise in construction.
Michael Yung Kam-hung, an assistant director of the Fire Services Department, said on Wednesday it would not take legal action against breaches discovered during Wang Fuk Court’s exterior overhaul, as it could not ascertain which item or arrangement was essential to the project.
“We do not know how it would affect the project’s progress if we arbitrarily decide that certain materials cannot be used,” he told an independent committee investigating the disaster at the estate.
Yung also said there was a tacit understanding among departments about which department should be responsible for addressing fire safety concerns.
He conceded that such a division of labour was not based on any legal provision or policy instruction, but stressed that cooperation between official branches had been established over the years and each department was clear about its responsibilities.

The witness rejected the notion that authorities’ failure to take action against the temporary use of polyfoam boards to seal windows exposed a gap in law enforcement.
He acknowledged an “obvious” loophole regarding the alteration to the estate’s emergency passages to enable workers’ access to scaffolding, as his department would not actively supervise changes made to building structures.
He stressed that whether such openings should be introduced to buildings under repair should be left to “the most professional department” to decide.
The inferno that raged across seven of eight blocks at the Tai Po residential complex for 43 hours from November 26 last year was the deadliest in Hong Kong since 1948, killing 168 people and displacing nearly 5,000.
The committee’s legal team previously identified six “human factors” contributing to the tragedy, including the use of substandard scaffolding mesh, polyfoam boards to seal windows and movable wooden boards to replace fireproof windows at the buildings’ staircases.
Yung, who specialises in fire safety, said complaints about flammable items should have been followed up by the Housing Bureau’s Independent Checking Unit, which oversees subsidised housing estates built under the Home Ownership Scheme.
He added that the problem of workers smoking on site was under the purview of the Labour Department.
Victor Dawes SC, the committee’s lead counsel, said the fire department was duty-bound to “give advice on fire protection measures and fire hazards as occasion requires” under the Fire Services Ordinance.

He pointed out that various departments were confused over their responsibilities in the absence of express documentation.
While the fire department insisted that polyfoam boards fell outside its jurisdiction, Independent Checking Unit surveyor Andy Ku Siu-ping also denied that his team was in charge, claiming that the Building (Construction) Regulation was not applicable to temporary structures.
Dawes argued that the discrepancy in understanding created a serious “regulatory vacuum”, but Yung said he believed the bureau’s unit had discharged its duties.
While acknowledging that the removal of windows at emergency staircases would have been a clear concern to fire authorities, Yung said officers were unaware of the practice at Wang Fuk Court before the blaze.
He added that the fire department’s action might contradict the view of the Labour Department, which might consider the temporary openings necessary from an occupational safety perspective.
“This was obviously a huge loophole,” Dawes said, adding that the fire force must be entitled to take action under the ordinance.
The lawyer also cited the Labour Department’s referral of a smoking complaint in July 2024 to the fire force, which had said the matter was not its concern.
“If government departments themselves are unsure about one another’s duties, we cannot expect residents to know who they should complain to,” he said.

Yung accepted the confusion was unacceptable, saying his department would no longer place the responsibility of relaying complaints to relevant authorities on residents.
The fire force’s new guidelines required officers to proactively refer public inquiries to the responsible departments and ensure all cases are followed up, the committee heard.
The inquiry also explored the necessity of repairing Wang Fuk Court’s fire water tanks, with previous evidence suggesting nobody had ever verified whether there was indeed a leakage.
Senior station officer Ng Wing-kwong said internal guidelines did not require officers to double-check whether a reported defect was major enough to justify the continued shutdown of fire hosepipes. He added that he was not in a position to explain why.
The hearing continues on Thursday at City Gallery in Central.

Founded in 2016, the company has deployed its driverless vehicles with 249 customers across six countries and regions, including at the Hong Kong International Airport and Singapore Changi Airport, according to its filing on Sunday.

Also applying under Chapter 18C is Shanghai Xizhi Technology. Founded in 2017 in the United States, it develops optical interconnect and optical computing products designed to accelerate artificial intelligence infrastructure. It plans to raise HK$2.53 billion and will start trading on April 28, according to its prospectus.
RobotPhoenix Intelligent Technology, established in 2012, makes industrial robots and automated solutions for light manufacturing sectors such as automotive, electronics and semiconductors.
Healthcare has been a key driver of liquidity growth on the Hong Kong stock market over the past decade, with trading turnover for the sector surging more than 11-fold between 2015 and 2025, outpacing the technology, media and telecommunications sector, which saw a 10-fold increase over the same period, according to HKEX data.

data.title: Hongkongers flock to theatres to snap up HK$30 Cinema Day tickets in advance
data.description:
Hong Kong residents, from housewives to stay-at-home mothers and couples, have rushed to theatres to secure HK$30 (US$4) tickets for Cinema Day, with many calling for even more discounts and family-friendly perks on other occasions.
Before sales opened at noon on Wednesday, some customers started queuing up from as early as 10.30am at Star Cinema in Tseung Kwan O to get discounted tickets for screenings this Saturday.
A housewife surnamed Ho was the first in line and hoped to get discounted tickets for the first time. She described the offer as attractive, adding that audiences were drawn to “high-quality films”. A standard cinema ticket typically costs HK$100.
Also in the queue were three mothers who had heard about the offer from friends and were also buying HK$30 tickets for the first time.
They said their children wanted to watch the animated adventure comedy, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie.
One of them, a 40-year-old stay-at-home mother surnamed Ng, said: “What children love most is films, and this is a good family activity. It would be nice if the government introduced more discounts targeting families or during festivals such as Children’s Day.”
Film-goers will be offered HK$30 tickets for almost 39,400 seats across the city on Cinema Day this Saturday.
Organiser Hong Kong Theatres Association launched Hong Kong Cinema Day in 2023, with sponsorship from the government, to support the industry’s recovery after three years of business disruptions and social-distancing measures during the Covid-19 pandemic.

Couple Larry and Jackie Yim, sci-fi fans who regularly watch films in the cinema, noted that there was no significant difference between ticket prices in Hong Kong and mainland China.
“The government should sponsor special events exclusive to Hong Kong, such as appreciation screenings, for movie lovers,” said Larry Yim, a 32-year-old salesman.
In the past three years, Cinema Day recorded a total of more than 600,000 admissions and over HK$44 million in box office revenue, according to the Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau.

data.title: Anthropic’s Mythos is stoking cybersecurity fears. What does it mean for China?
data.description:
In the first of a three-part series on Anthropic’s new powerful Mythos AI model, we look at its impact on Chinese AI, cybersecurity and competition with the US.
US start-up Anthropic announced its latest artificial intelligence model Claude Mythos Preview on April 7, sparking an unprecedented global response among policymakers and regulators due to its powerful ability to identify and exploit cybersecurity vulnerabilities.
Instead of a public release, Anthropic released Mythos to a consortium of US companies including Cisco, JPMorgan Chase and Nvidia to use the model to secure their critical software in an initiative called Project Glasswing.
The US National Security Agency and other US governmental bodies have already begun using the model, according to US media reports. No Chinese companies were invited to use the model to patch their cybersecurity vulnerabilities given Anthropic’s labelling of China as an “adversarial nation”, while the company’s services continue to be banned in greater China, including Hong Kong.
How do Chinese models compare?
According to Beijing-based consultancy Concordia AI, China’s primarily open-source models still lag closed-source US models in their cyber capabilities. However, the rapid progress of Chinese models over the past year has meant that their capabilities are also improving rapidly.
One of the key benchmarks that Anthropic tested Mythos on was “CyberGym”, which tests AI agents on their ability to find previously discovered vulnerabilities in real open-source software projects.
According to Anthropic, Mythos achieved a new high score on CyberGym of 83.1 per cent. The same day, Beijing-based Zhipu AI released its latest model GLM 5.1, which it said achieved a CyberGym score of 68.7 per cent, surpassing the score achieved by Anthropic’s previous flagship model, Claude Opus 4.6, which was released two months earlier in February.

Another Chinese model that has publicly revealed its CyberGym performance is Moonshot AI’s Kimi K2.5, released in late January, which achieved a self-reported score of 41.3 per cent.
Earlier this month, researchers from the US, UK and Israel published independent evaluations of the model, concluding that it had yet to possess cutting-edge autonomous offence capabilities, “the phase most critical to real-world offensive impact”.
Still, they noted that the model’s competence in “semi-automated” cyber offence warranted “continued monitoring as capabilities in autonomous exploitation may improve in future iterations”.
The Beijing-based start-up’s latest open-source model, Kimi K2.6, which was released on Monday, has not disclosed its CyberGym performance. Third-party benchmark firm Artificial Analysis said upon the model’s release that it was the strongest open-source model in the world. Moonshot did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
However, efforts are also under way in China to use AI to bolster cybersecurity defence, as AI cybersecurity capabilities are dual use, meaning that they can be used for both offence and defence.
For instance, researchers at Alibaba Group Holding are developing a tool called the “Qwen cyber agent” to help firms and developers defend critical software. The project is co-led by Yuchong Xie, an AI researcher at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, who previously led cybersecurity evaluations for Moonshot’s Kimi K2.5.
The company has not announced a release date for the tool, and it remains unclear whether it will be made public. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

What are the implications for US-China AI competition?
Chinese authorities and companies have largely stayed mum about Mythos. Meanwhile, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said at a recent event that Mythos represented a breakthrough for the US in its AI competition with China, while Anthropic rival OpenAI has also indicated that it will soon release a model of equivalent capability.
Given the global threat posed by cybersecurity vulnerabilities in critical software infrastructure, Mythos has sparked debate about whether its defence capabilities should be shared with China.
In a podcast interview last week, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said the development of Mythos warranted a rethink of aggressive US tech policy on China, notably its export controls on advanced semiconductor chips. Instead, the US should have more dialogue with China, which could easily develop a Mythos of its own, he said.
“Victimising [China], turning them into an enemy, likely isn’t the best answer,” said Huang. “We want the United States to win, but I think having a dialogue and having research dialogue is probably the safest thing to do.”
How are other countries responding?
The emergence of Mythos has also raised alarm in Asia, where ministers and central bankers have raced to warn industries to review their cybersecurity apparatus to mitigate risks.
Several financial regulators in the region, including South Korea, Australia and Singapore, have said that they are working with industry to respond to the new threats.
On Monday, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority told Reuters that it was engaging with major banks on tackling evolving AI cybersecurity threats, including the formation of a new dedicated public-private sector task force to address growing risks.

data.title: How China’s ‘landmark’ drug pricing guidelines could be a boon for biotech
data.description:
The State Council, China’s cabinet, has published guidelines on innovative drug pricing that a bank analyst has described as the “most significant” in a decade, as they aim to reward drug innovation and ease pricing tension for domestic and multinational pharmaceutical companies.
The framework document published this month said patented innovative drugs would be supported through value-based pricing, while the prices of generic drugs would be driven down through market competition and volume-based procurement.
Under volume-based procurement, the government buys drugs in bulk for public hospitals, putting multinational companies under intense price and competitive pressure.
The policy allows biotech companies to charge higher prices in the private market, including at private hospitals and retail pharmacies, and online.
The document also called for commercial insurance to play a bigger role in funding innovative drugs.
“This provides more profitable channels to innovative drug makers and potentially benefits internet healthcare players, pharmacies and private healthcare services, such as Alibaba Health, Yifeng and Hygeia,” John Yung, head of Asia healthcare research at Citigroup, wrote in a research report released on April 16.
Alibaba Health Information Technology – known as Alibaba Health for short – is the flagship healthcare platform of Alibaba Group Holding, which also owns the South China Morning Post.
Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals, CSPC Pharmaceutical Group, Innovent Biologics and other multinational drug makers that sold innovative medicines in China stood to benefit from the new pricing framework, Yung said.
The Hang Seng Healthcare Index, which tracks some of China’s most innovative pharmaceutical companies, including Akeso and Innovent, climbed from 3881.91 on April 14, the day the guidelines were released, to a peak of 4060.89 before retreating to 3,909.82 by 10.20am on Wednesday – a gain of 0.72 per cent.
Hong Kong’s benchmark Hang Seng Index rose about 1.1 per cent over the same period.
In his report, Yung said the document was “landmark national guidance” and “the most significant central government guidance on drug pricing since 2015”, adding that it demonstrated “a clear government commitment to fostering a robust and globally competitive domestic pharmaceutical industry”.
It focuses more on encouraging innovations from the pricing and reimbursement angle
Bruce Liu, Simon-Kucher
Bruce Liu, senior partner at global consulting firm Simon-Kucher, said the guideline’s support of the biopharma industry’s development was “in line with the 15th five-year plan, prioritising it as a strategic pillar going forwards”.
“It didn’t specify local or multinational companies, so there is no favouritism – at least on paper – instead it focuses more on encouraging innovations from the pricing and reimbursement angle,” Liu said, adding that the goal was to encourage innovations on both the supply and demand sides, although “more specifics are yet to be seen”.
Wang Xiaoning, who oversees pharmaceutical pricing at the National Healthcare Security Administration, told a news conference last week that the administration was developing policies to let drug makers self-evaluate their drugs’ clinical value and innovation level to justify a launch price commensurate with their development costs, with price stability guaranteed for a set period.
However, Linda Shu, head of China healthcare research at HSBC, said China’s commercial insurance list for innovative drugs, introduced late last year, remained nascent.
“It will be a trend for China’s innovative drugs to grow [through commercial insurance] in the future,” she said. “But in the near term, they are still testing whether it can grow as fast as the national reimbursement list.”
Beijing listed biomedicine as an “emerging pillar industry”, alongside integrated circuits, aerospace and the low-altitude economy, for the first time in the government work report delivered to the national legislature on March 5.
Chinese biotech firms reached a record 157 agreements to out-licence early-stage drugs to global partners last year, with the deals worth a total of US$135.7 billion.

data.title: First local dengue case could lead to outbreak in coming months: health official
data.description:
The first local case of dengue fever recorded for the first time in more than a year in Hong Kong indicates that undiagnosed imported cases could lead to an outbreak in the coming months, a health official has warned, while stressing that the risk was preventable.
The warning by Albert Au Ka-wing, head of the Centre for Health Protection’s communicable disease branch, came after one case was recorded in Tai Po last Thursday, in a patient with no travel history.
“The first local dengue fever case proved the existence of possibly undiagnosed cases in Hong Kong in which people were bitten by mosquitoes. They infected the mosquitoes, which then spread the virus, indicating a risk of local transmission,” Au said on a radio programme on Wednesday.
“The increase in temperature and arrival of the rainy season will create ideal breeding grounds for mosquitoes. With the mosquito-borne disease present in Southeast Asia and neighbouring areas … we cannot ignore the risk of an outbreak, but it is preventable and under control for now.”
Hong Kong has recorded 13 dengue fever cases so far this year, of which 12 were imported. The city recorded no local cases last year.
The recent case involved a 21-year-old man with no underlying health conditions, who developed a fever, headache, myalgia and a rash on April 12. He sought medical attention at Alice Ho Miu Ling Nethersole Hospital in Tai Po on April 16, according to the authorities.
The patient was admitted for treatment and is currently in stable condition. His blood sample tested positive for the dengue virus, and he reported being bitten by mosquitoes near his workplace, a road construction site near Long Yan Road in Penny’s Bay on Lantau Island.
He did not recall being bitten by mosquitoes anywhere else.
He had no travel history during the incubation period between March 29 and April 9, and stayed mainly in the vicinity of the construction site in Penny’s Bay and his home in Tai Po’s Tai Wo Estate.
His household contacts and colleagues at the same construction site are currently asymptomatic, with the government finding no epidemiological link between this case and other previously recorded imported cases in Hong Kong.
Au said work at the Penny’s Bay construction site would be temporarily suspended until mosquito control and other preventive measures were completed at the site and its surrounding area.

Environmental inspections found a small amount of stagnant water in containers and equipment in the area near the site, with some drainage channels clogged with weeds and dead leaves.
Tai Wo Estate residents have been offered dengue assessments at a health station set up in Tai Wo Plaza, which includes blood tests for those who display symptoms, and provides other preventive measures.
Last year, 59 dengue fever cases were recorded, all of which were imported. In 2024, 161 cases were recorded, including 156 imported cases and five local ones.
Separately, Au highlighted the five measles cases recorded in Hong Kong so far this year, with two imported from Indonesia, one local case, and the remaining two linked to one of the imported cases.
While the increase in cases was notable – the city recorded just one measles case last year – he stressed that the World Health Organization confirmed that Hong Kong had eradicated measles in 2016, and that the rate of testing positive for measles antibodies in the population was consistently above 95 per cent.
“There is a low likelihood of a widespread measles outbreak in Hong Kong. But given that the situation is deteriorating in many parts of the world at present, and that Hong Kong is popular among tourists, there is still a risk for imported cases,” Au said, pledging to monitor the situation closely.

data.title: Hong Kong’s MTR Corp sells record HK$18.8 billion in green bonds
data.description:
The MTR Corporation has sold HK$18.8 billion (US$2.4 billion) in green bonds, the largest and the company’s first public bond issuance in the Hong Kong dollar market, in its latest efforts to diversify funding sources for a string of projects.
The rail giant said on Wednesday that it sold five-year, 10-year and 30-year bonds, with the size of each tranche also representing the largest for their respective tenors to date.
The overall order book of the issue exceeded HK$60 billion, or three times the total issue size, reflecting strong demand, the semi-privatised corporation said.
The more than HK$8.3 billion five-year tranche, HK$7.5 billion 10-year tranche and HK$3 billion 30-year tranche were priced with coupons of 2.88 per cent, 3.30 per cent and 4.00 per cent respectively, the corporation said.
The 30-year tranche appealed strongly to long-term institutional investors such as retirement fund managers, allowing citizens to support the city’s infrastructure development, it added.
“The latest issuance is part of an ongoing, forward-looking financing strategy to leverage favourable market conditions in the capital markets to support the development of major railway infrastructure construction for Hong Kong’s future,” the MTR Corp’s CEO Jeny Yeung Mei-chun said.
“Financial flexibility, together with the MTR Corporation’s long-standing prudent financial management and rigorous project management, will continue to guide us through our continuous efforts to implement the new projects.”
The rail giant pointed out that proceeds from the issuance would support eligible green investments and advance future railway construction in the city.
Green bonds are fixed-income financial products used to raise capital for environmentally sustainable infrastructure projects and business activities.
Yeung added that upon completion, the new projects “will deliver an even more connected and low-carbon public transport service for Hong Kong, and at the same time create new growth drivers for the business, generating value for shareholders and all stakeholders”.

data.title: Residents leave prayers and flowers on third day of return to Wang Fuk Court
data.description:
The last batch of residents who briefly returned to their flats in Hong Kong’s Wang Fuk Court under a special government arrangement said prayers and left flower bouquets as a final farewell to their fire-ravaged homes.
The residents, who were among 79 households that registered to visit Wang Sun House on Wednesday morning, arrived at the building with trolleys, backpacks and bags, some armed with stools to aid their arduous climb up at least 20 floors.
An elderly woman surnamed Tang, who lived on the 24th floor, said she and her friends would pray together before making their ascent.
“There’s no goodbye to be said up there. I just hope to retrieve my cat’s ashes,” she said.
Tang said her dead 19-year-old cat’s ashes were stored in a china jar and kept within a stainless steel box.
“Its melting point is supposed to be around 1,500 degrees Celsius. I think it hasn’t melted yet.”
The deadly inferno last November killed 168 people, displaced about 5,000 residents and destroyed all but one of Wang Fuk Court’s eight blocks.
Under the return scheme, residents are allowed three hours in their flats, excluding time spent on the stairs. Up to four people per household may return at a time.
About 6,000 residents have registered with the government to visit their former homes and collect belongings.
Another male resident with a plastic bag and backpack carried flowers on his way up to his former home.
Mandy Wong, who would be walking up to her 25th floor home with her elder sister and mother, said she had prepared herself mentally for the trip.
“My feelings are a bit different from my mother’s. I don’t have many valuables there, and I kind of hope to declutter as well,” Wong, who is in her 40s, said.
She planned to take pictures of the flat as a way to bid a final farewell to her former home and to her dog, which died in the fire.
As residents would have to walk up 20 to 30 flights of stairs to reach their upper floor homes on Wednesday, some elderly residents chose not to make the trip.

An 80-year-old resident, surnamed Leung, said the climb to her 30th floor home would be too strenuous for her.
“I’ve tried, but I wouldn’t be able to come down even if I could go up. My legs would be shaking,” she said, adding that her son-in-law would make the trip.
A resident in her 70s, surnamed Ng, said she lived on a middle floor but was unable to return home due to poor health.
But others in her family would go on her behalf. They hoped to retrieve photographs of her children, if any remained.
“I hope to go up in the near future if my condition allows,” she said, despite holding little hope of recovering other valuable items from her flat.
Another resident, surnamed Tsoi, brought along some red-white-and-blue-striped nylon bags, but said he had been following the news closely and was mentally prepared that there would be nothing to retrieve from his home.
Residents from 154 households on the lower and middle floors of Wang Sun House visited on Monday and Tuesday, while those living on higher floors returned on Wednesday, the final group in the block.

They were given helmets and protective gloves before entering the building, which has no electricity.
Residents began returning to Wang Fuk Court on Monday to retrieve belongings, with 264 people from 77 households visiting Wang Sun House that day.
On Tuesday, one father found a single Gundam figure belonging to his son, while a wedding photographer retrieved the charred remains of his Leica camera, which he said was worth HK$600,000 (US$76,600).
Some residents strapped on exoskeleton assistive devices to aid their climb, while others used metal detectors to search for melted metals and gold in their homes.
Families from Wang Cheong House, Wang Yan House and Wang Tao House will return between April 23 and 28.
Time slots for residents in the remaining three blocks – Wang Tai House, Wang Kin House and Wang Shing House – are scheduled between April 29 and May 4.
Arrangements for Wang Cheong House and Wang Tai House, the more severely damaged blocks, will take six days to complete, with access limited to five floors per day. The other buildings will be open for three days.

data.title: Hong Kong IPOs lure mainland China insurers seeking higher returns
data.description:
Mainland China’s insurance companies have emerged as major cornerstone investors in Hong Kong initial public offerings (IPOs) as they seek higher returns and benefit from Beijing’s policy support for insurers to invest in equity markets.
Ping An Insurance (Group), Taikang Life Insurance, New China Life Insurance, Dajia Life Insurance and China Post Life Insurance were among the key players that invested more than US$250 million across about 11 IPOs so far this year, according to UBS data.
Their investment accelerated, reaching about 75 per cent of the US$333 million committed for the whole of last year.
“We started to see more active participation of mainland insurers in 2026,” said John Lee Chen-kwok, vice-chairman and co-head of Asia coverage at UBS in Hong Kong. “They are overall driving up equity investment given the very low rate environment in mainland China.”
The insurers mainly invested in new-economy stocks such as artificial intelligence, semiconductors, new energy and biotechnology, said Tom Chan Pak-lam, honorary president of the Institute of Securities Dealers.
Chan said mainland insurers’ interest in Hong Kong IPOs was driven by regulatory changes, their pursuit of higher returns and strategic alignment. “The Chinese government has been actively pushing insurers to become ‘patient capital’ as they can invest [with a long-term outlook], which provides a stable funding source for the market,” he said.

Mainland insurance companies held total assets of 41.31 trillion yuan (US$6.1 trillion) at the end of last year, according to government statistics. As some of the largest institutional investors in the equity markets, they had been positioning for long-term commitments, typically spanning a decade or more.
Beijing has rolled out a number of measures since early 2025, including relaxing capital requirements, to allow mainland insurers to invest between 30 and 40 per cent of their assets in stocks, up from 10 to 20 per cent previously, depending on their asset size.
Some 20 securities firms, fund managers and insurance companies received 55 billion yuan in liquid assets in January last year from the People’s Bank of China through a swap facility that could be used as collateral for borrowing to buy stocks.
“As interest rates have been falling in the past two years, traditional bond investments and bank deposits delivered lower returns, which prompted insurers to seek higher-return assets such as stock investments,” Chan said.
The mainland insurers’ bets on certain IPOs have proved rewarding. Taikang Life Insurance and Sunshine Life Insurance were cornerstone investors in the IPO of Manycore Tech, a design software developer hailed as one of Hangzhou’s “Six Little Dragons”.

The two insurers invested a total of HK$181 million in Manycore, according to the company’s listing prospectus. Its shares jumped 172 per cent at the open on its trading debut in Hong Kong on Friday.
“The increase in mainland insurance companies investing in Hong Kong IPOs and stock markets is due to the market rally in recent years that has brought good returns to insurers,” said Kenny Ng Lai-yin, a strategist at Everbright Securities International.
The benchmark Hang Seng Index rose 28 per cent last year, after jumping 18 per cent in 2024, following a 14 per cent fall in 2023.
Ng said mainland insurers were long-term investors, so their participation in IPOs could help the companies maintain stable share prices while adding to overall market liquidity.
Kenny Tang Sing-hing, chairman of the Hong Kong Institute of Financial Analysts and Professional Commentators, said mainland insurers were likely to continue investing in Hong Kong IPOs.
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing’s (HKEX) main board retained its title as the world’s top IPO market in the first quarter, after reclaiming the spot last year for the first time since 2019.
HKEX data pointed to a steady IPO pipeline, with 16 new and refiled applications received on March 31. The exchange has received 208 new filings this year, with 413 cases still under review.
“The strong IPO performance of the Hong Kong market has provided investment opportunities for mainland insurance companies,” Tang said. “As the interest rate environment on the mainland remains low, the insurers cannot get good returns by investing in bank deposits. They will continue to invest in Hong Kong IPOs in future.”

data.title: CATL to drive electrification with faster-charging, higher-performing batteries
data.description:
Contemporary Amperex Technology Limited (CATL), the world’s largest electric vehicle (EV) battery producer, has unveiled its upgraded technologies – including lighter battery packs and faster charging mode – which are expected to accelerate the global automotive industry’s electrification drive.
The company, which holds more than a 40 per cent share of the global market, aimed to set a healthy tone for the Chinese EV sector to implement its go-global strategy, focusing on constant technological advancements rather than an endless cycle of price competition, according to founder and chairman Robin Zeng Yuqun.
“For Chinese EV technologies [to] expand abroad, we should chase, not only the speed and scale, but the quality of innovation, the ability to validate and the credibility of the brands,” he said at the company’s Super Technology Day event in Beijing on Tuesday. “A rigorous attitude towards scientific research is our most important commitment today.”
At the event, CATL announced that its third-generation Shenxing battery took six minutes and 27 seconds to charge, down from 10 per cent to 98 per cent. The charging speed beats BYD’s second-generation Blade battery which takes nine minutes to charge from 10 per cent to 90 per cent.
CATL claimed that it was the fastest EV charging technology in the world.
BYD, the world’s largest EV assembler, also produces battery packs. Its global market share stood at 13.4 per cent in the first two months of 2026, trailing only CATL, according to Seoul-based SNE Research.
In practice, faster charging battery technology further eases drivers’ range anxiety since it reduces the time they need to spend at charging stations.
CATL also announced its newly designed Qilin battery pack, weighing 625kg, could offer a car as much as 1,000km driving range.
Before, a pack capable of driving a car for 1,000km on a single charge would be 255kg heavier because it used more battery cells, Gao Huan, chief technology officer of CATL, said.


At Tuesday’s event CATL added that its sodium-ion batteries, an alternative to lithium-ion batteries, would be mass-produced and delivered in the fourth quarter of this year to power some of the electric cars built by state-owned Changan Automobile.
Sodium-ion technology uses a more widely available raw material and the batteries are cheaper to produce.
CATL, based in Ningde, east China’s Fujian province, reported a net profit of 20.74 billion yuan (US$3.04 billion) in the three months to March, up 48.5 per cent year on year. The figure beat a Bloomberg consensus estimate of 17.6 billion yuan.
It also planned to earmark 30 billion yuan to establish a subsidiary to manage and expand mining assets, after the global energy shock paved the way for a quicker entry into the world’s automotive and energy storage system markets, CATL said in a filing to the Shenzhen Stock Exchange.
Chinese assemblers and automotive supply-chain vendors currently stand at the vanguard of EV technology and production, buoyed by government support and consumers’ willingness to embrace innovation, according to analysts.
Over the past few years, they slashed prices of their products when vying for a bigger market share outside mainland China despite their superiority to overseas peers.
Beijing stepped in last year to stop vicious price competition among EV makers, warning of penalties for firms that spearheaded discount wars.
Carmakers are now taking one of two paths: upgrading production techniques to reduce manufacturing costs and make their prices more attractive, or offering consumers new models that outperform existing ones at the same price, according to Nick Lai, head of auto research for Asia-Pacific at JPMorgan.

data.title: Tai Po fire probe: ‘regulatory vacuum’ revealed in polyfoam board oversight
data.description:
In his opening statement, committee lead counsel Victor Dawes identified six “human factors” that led to the almost complete failure of fire safety measures at Wang Fuk Court.
The hearing on Tuesday touched on four of them: workers smoking on site, the use of flammable styrofoam boards, the installation of movable wooden boards on emergency staircases and the use of substandard, non-fire-retardant scaffolding mesh.
The other two factors Dawes earlier highlighted were the deactivation of the fire alarm system and the drained firefighting water tanks across all eight buildings at Wang Fuk Court.

data.title: Scammers trick overseas students out of gold in fake money-laundering probes
data.description:
Fraudsters have duped students studying abroad out of up to HK$1.5 million (US$191,531) each by accusing them of money laundering and luring them to Hong Kong to buy gold for bogus law-enforcement investigations, prompting a police warning.
At least nine victims aged between 19 and 26, who are studying in the United Kingdom, Australia and other countries, have recently fallen prey to such cross-border impersonation scams, according to Hong Kong police.
“Their losses ranged from HK$740,000 to HK$1.57 million,” the force’s Anti-Deception Coordination Centre said on its website.
Police did not reveal whether the victims were mainland or Hong Kong students, but they said the scammers had disguised themselves as officers from Shanghai’s public security bureau and Hong Kong’s Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) in these cases.
Posing as mainland officials, the scammers claimed the students were involved in money laundering or other criminal offences and demanded that they cooperate in investigations.
The victims were instructed to fly to Hong Kong immediately and go to jewellery stores to buy gold pellets, the centre said in its latest scam alert.
After the purchases, the scammers switched identities, posing as ICAC officers, and arranged in-person meetings with the victims in the city to collect the gold before disappearing.
The victims later realised they had been deceived and called police.
The force stressed that no law enforcement agency in Hong Kong, mainland China or overseas would ever instruct anyone to buy gold, hand over cash or surrender valuables for “investigative purposes”.
“Anyone claiming to be a law enforcer and requesting the in-person handover of property or unreasonable transfers is almost certainly a scammer,” police said.
The force urged the public to terminate suspicious calls immediately and verify any such claims through the ICAC’s 2526 6366 hotline or the Anti-Scam 18222 helpline.
Separately, the force’s cybersecurity and technology crime bureau urged the public to remain vigilant against romance-investment scams, also known as “pig-butchering” after a 61-year-old retired man lost more than HK$2.5 million.
The victim met a “woman” on Facebook who claimed to be an information technology professional based in Taiwan.
“The pair exchanged daily messages, shared personal details, and soon addressed each other affectionately as ‘baby’,” police said.
After the relationship developed, the scammer introduced him to investment opportunities, claiming expertise in the US stock market, and directed him to a bogus website.
The woman told the retiree that the site featured an analysis system powered by artificial intelligence that offered precise investment tips and “high-return, low-risk” strategies.
On her advice, the man made multiple transfers totalling more than HK$2.5 million to personal bank accounts provided by the scammer for investments.
He later realised he had been defrauded when he could no longer access the website.
In the second week of this month, police handled more than 80 reports of online investment fraud involving losses of more than HK$80 million.
